title: PHP Intro
content:
PHP Introduction

Server-Side Scripting

PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor) is a powerful server-side scripting language designed specifically for web development. Unlike HTML, CSS, or JavaScript (which run in the user's browser), PHP code is executed on the server, and the plain HTML result is sent back to the browser.



  • Generates dynamic page content.
  • Can create, open, read, write, and close files on the server.
  • Can collect form data and send/receive cookies.
  • Widely used with databases like MySQL to build robust web applications.

title: Setup
content:
PHP Setup & Installation

Local Server Environment

Because PHP runs on a server, you cannot just open a PHP file in your browser like an HTML file. To run PHP locally on your computer, you need a local server environment.

  • XAMPP, WAMP, or MAMP: These are free software bundles that install a web server (Apache), a database (MySQL), and PHP on your computer all at once.
  • File Extension: PHP files must be saved with the .php extension.

title: PHP Syntax
content:
PHP Syntax

Embedded in HTML

A PHP script can be placed anywhere in a document. It starts with <?php and ends with ?>. Every PHP statement must end with a semicolon (;).

Basic Syntax
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>

  <h1>My First PHP Page</h1>

  <?php
    // This is a single-line comment in PHP
    echo "Hello World!";
  ?>

</body>
</html>

title: Variables
content:
PHP Variables

The $ Sign

In PHP, a variable starts with the $ sign, followed by the name of the variable. PHP is loosely typed, meaning you do not need to declare data types (like `int` or `string`) before assigning a value.

Declaring Variables
<?php
  $name = "Sanjith";
  $age = 20;
  
  echo "My name is " . $name . " and I am " . $age . " years old.";
?>

title: Data Types
content:
PHP Data Types

Variable Values

Variables can store data of different types, and different data types can do different things.

  • String: Text, e.g., "Hello"
  • Integer: Whole numbers, e.g., 42
  • Float: Decimal numbers, e.g., 3.14
  • Boolean: true or false
  • Array: Multiple values in one variable.
  • Object: Instances of classes.
  • NULL: A variable with no value assigned.

title: Echo & Print
content:
Echo and Print

Outputting Data

In PHP, there are two basic ways to get output: echo and print. They are almost identical, but echo is marginally faster and is the industry standard used by most developers.

Output Examples
<?php
  $txt = "PHP";
  
  echo "I love $txt!<br>";
  print "This is output using print.";
?>

title: Operators
content:
PHP Operators

Math and Logic

Operators are used to perform operations on variables and values.

  • Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (Modulo)
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=
  • Comparison: == (Equal), === (Identical), != (Not equal)
  • Logical: && (And), || (Or), ! (Not)

title: If...Else
content:
If...Else Statements

Conditional Logic

Conditional statements are used to perform different actions based on different conditions.

If/Else Syntax
<?php
  $age = 18;
  
  if ($age >= 18) {
    echo "You are an Adult.";
  } else {
    echo "You are a Minor.";
  }
?>

title: Switch
content:
Switch Statement

Multiple Conditions

The switch statement is used to perform different actions based on different conditions. Use it to select one of many blocks of code to be executed.

Switch Syntax
<?php
  $color = "red";
  
  switch ($color) {
    case "red": 
      echo "Red selected"; 
      break;
    case "blue":
      echo "Blue selected";
      break;
    default: 
      echo "Other color";
  }
?>

title: Loops
content:
PHP Loops

Repeating Code

Often when you write code, you want the same block of code to run over and over again. PHP supports for, while, do...while, and foreach loops.

For Loop
<?php
  for ($i = 0; $i < 5; $i++) {
    echo "The number is: $i <br>";
  }
?>

title: Arrays
content:
PHP Arrays

Storing Multiple Values

An array stores multiple values in one single variable. PHP supports indexed arrays, associative arrays (key-value pairs), and multidimensional arrays.



Array Examples
<?php
  // Indexed Array
  $fruits = array("Apple", "Banana", "Cherry");
  echo "I like " . $fruits[0] . "<br>";
  
  // Associative Array
  $ages = array("Peter"=>"35", "Ben"=>"37");
  echo "Peter is " . $ages['Peter'] . " years old.";
?>

title: Functions
content:
PHP Functions

Reusable Blocks

Besides the built-in PHP functions, it is possible to create your own functions. A function is a block of statements that can be used repeatedly in a program.

Defining Functions
<?php
  function greet($name) {
    return "Hello, " . $name . "!";
  }
  
  echo greet("Sanjith");
?>

title: Forms Handling
content:
Handling Forms

Processing User Data

When a user submits an HTML form, PHP can collect that data using the superglobals $_GET and $_POST. $_POST is used for secure or large data (like passwords), while $_GET places the data in the URL.



Reading POST Data
<!-- In your HTML file: -->
<!-- <form method="POST" action="welcome.php">
     Name: <input type="text" name="username">
     <input type="submit">
     </form> -->

<?php
  // Inside welcome.php:
  if ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] == "POST") {
    $name = $_POST['username'];
    echo "Welcome, " . htmlspecialchars($name);
  }
?>

title: Sessions
content:
PHP Sessions

Tracking Users

A session is a way to store information (in variables) to be used across multiple pages. Unlike a cookie, the information is stored securely on the server, not on the user's computer.

Starting a Session
<?php
  // session_start() MUST be the very first thing in your document
  session_start();
  
  // Set session variables
  $_SESSION["user"] = "Sanjith";
  $_SESSION["role"] = "Admin";
  
  echo "Session variables are set.";
?>

title: Cookies
content:
PHP Cookies

Client-Side Storage

A cookie is often used to identify a user. A cookie is a small file that the server embeds on the user's computer. Each time the same computer requests a page, it will send the cookie too.

Setting a Cookie
<?php
  // setcookie(name, value, expire, path)
  // Expires in 1 hour (3600 seconds)
  setcookie("user", "Sanjith", time() + 3600, "/");
  
  if(isset($_COOKIE["user"])) {
    echo "Welcome back, " . $_COOKIE["user"];
  } else {
    echo "Cookie is not set!";
  }
?>

title: File Handling
content:
File Handling

Managing Server Files

File handling is an important part of any web application. PHP has several functions for creating, reading, uploading, and editing files on the server.

Reading a File
<?php
  // Open the file in "r" (read) mode
  $file = fopen("test.txt", "r") or die("Unable to open file!");
  
  // Output the file contents
  echo fread($file, filesize("test.txt"));
  
  // Always close the file when you are done!
  fclose($file);
?>

title: MySQL Connection
content:
Connecting to MySQL

The Database Link

With PHP, you can connect to and manipulate databases. MySQL is the most popular database system used with PHP. You can connect using the mysqli extension or PDO.



MySQLi Object-Oriented
<?php
  $servername = "localhost";
  $username = "root";
  $password = "";
  $dbname = "myDB";

  // Create connection
  $conn = new mysqli($servername, $username, $password, $dbname);

  // Check connection
  if ($conn->connect_error) {
    die("Connection failed: " . $conn->connect_error);
  }
  echo "Connected successfully";
?>

title: CRUD Operations
content:
CRUD Operations

Database Mastery

CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, and Delete. Once connected to a MySQL database, PHP uses SQL queries (like INSERT INTO, SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE) to perform these four essential database operations.

title: OOP Basics
content:
OOP in PHP

Object-Oriented PHP

From PHP 5 onwards, you can write object-oriented code. A Class is a template for objects, and an object is an instance of a class.

Class and Object
<?php
  class Car {
    public $brand;
    
    function setBrand($name) {
      $this->brand = $name;
    }
    function getBrand() {
      return $this->brand;
    }
  }

  $myCar = new Car();
  $myCar->setBrand("Toyota");
  echo "My car is a " . $myCar->getBrand();
?>

title: PHP Summary
content:
PHP Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

You have successfully conquered the fundamentals of PHP! You now understand how a server-side language bridges the gap between the frontend UI and the backend database.

  • Syntax & Flow: Variables, Loops, Functions, and Arrays.
  • State Management: Tracking users with Sessions and Cookies.
  • Data Handling: Collecting Form data and File manipulation.
  • Databases: Connecting to MySQL and executing queries.

With PHP under your belt, you can confidently build dynamic, database-driven web applications from scratch!

title: Superglobals
content:
PHP Superglobals

Global Data Access

Superglobals are built-in variables that are always accessible, regardless of scope. They provide data from forms, sessions, cookies, and the server environment itself. The most common are $_GET, $_POST, $_SESSION, $_COOKIE, $_SERVER, and $_REQUEST.



Using $_REQUEST
<?php
  // $_REQUEST is an array that contains the contents of $_GET, $_POST, and $_COOKIE.
  // It is a quick way to grab data sent by the user!
  
  if ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] == "POST") {
    $name = $_REQUEST['username'];
    if (empty($name)) {
      echo "Name is empty";
    } else {
      echo "Hello, " . htmlspecialchars($name);
    }
  }
?>

title: Assoc Arrays
content:
Indexed & Associative Arrays

Key-Value Pairs

PHP arrays can hold multiple values. Indexed arrays use numeric keys (0, 1, 2), while associative arrays use named keys (like a dictionary). You can easily loop through both types using a foreach loop.

Foreach Loop
<?php
  $cars = array("Volvo", "BMW", "Toyota"); // Indexed
  $ages = array("Peter"=>35, "Ben"=>37, "Joe"=>43); // Associative
  
  // Looping through an associative array
  foreach($ages as $name => $age) {
    echo "$name is $age years old.<br>";
  }
?>

title: Multi-D Arrays
content:
Multidimensional Arrays

Arrays Inside Arrays

PHP supports arrays containing other arrays, allowing for 2D, 3D, and even deeper nested structures. These are incredibly useful for storing tabular data (like rows and columns from a database).



2D Array Access
<?php
  // A 2D array storing Cars: Brand, Stock, Sold
  $myArray = array(
    array("Volvo", 22, 18),
    array("BMW", 15, 13),
    array("Saab", 5, 2)
  );
  
  // Accessing row 0, column 0 (Volvo)
  echo $myArray[0][0] . ": In stock: " . $myArray[0][1];
?>

title: Array Functions
content:
PHP Array Functions

Manipulating Data

PHP provides an extensive list of built-in array functions like array_merge(), array_push(), array_pop(), and array_count_values(). These functions help you manipulate and search arrays efficiently without writing manual loops.

Array Push & Merge
<?php
  $arr1 = array("Red", "Green");
  
  // Push adds an item to the end of the array
  array_push($arr1, "Blue");
  
  $arr2 = array("Yellow", "Purple");
  
  // Merge combines two arrays together
  $combined = array_merge($arr1, $arr2);
  
  print_r($combined); // print_r is great for viewing array contents!
?>

title: Functions & Params
content:
Functions & Parameter Handling

Advanced Functions

Functions are reusable blocks of code. PHP supports advanced parameter handling, including default parameter values (if the user forgets to pass an argument) and passing by reference (modifying the original variable directly).

Default Parameters
<?php
  // $minheight has a default value of 50
  function setHeight($minheight = 50) {
    echo "The height is : $minheight <br>";
  }
  
  setHeight(350); // Outputs 350
  setHeight();    // Outputs 50 (uses default)
?>

title: Classes & Objects
content:
PHP OOP – Classes and Objects

Modeling Data

Object-oriented programming (OOP) allows you to model real-world data using classes. A class defines properties and methods, acting as a blueprint. Objects are the actual, usable instances built from that blueprint.



The __construct() Method
<?php
  class Car {
    public $model;
    
    // The constructor runs automatically when the object is created
    function __construct($m) {
      $this->model = $m;
    }
  }
  
  $myCar = new Car("Volvo XC90");
  echo "I drive a " . $myCar->model;
?>

title: Traits
content:
PHP Traits

Overcoming Single Inheritance

PHP only supports single inheritance (a child class can only inherit from one single parent). Traits solve this by allowing you to declare reusable methods that can be included in multiple, completely unrelated classes.

Using Traits
<?php
  trait Logger {
    public function logMsg($msg) {
      echo "LOG: " . $msg . "<br>";
    }
  }
  
  class User {
    use Logger; // Inject the trait methods!
  }
  
  $u = new User();
  $u->logMsg("User logged in.");
?>

title: Cookies Deep Dive
content:
Cookies Deep Dive

Browser Storage

Cookies are small text files stored in the user's browser that persist between requests. They are perfect for remembering non-sensitive user preferences (like a dark mode toggle). Use setcookie() to create them and the $_COOKIE superglobal to read them.



Cookie Expiration
<?php
  // Sets a cookie named "user" that expires in 1 day (86400 seconds)
  setcookie("user", "John Doe", time() + 86400, "/");
  
  if(isset($_COOKIE["user"])) {
    echo "Cookie is active! Value: " . $_COOKIE["user"];
  }
?>

title: Sessions Deep Dive
content:
Sessions Deep Dive

Server-Side State

While Cookies store data in the browser, Sessions store data securely on the server. When you start a session, the server generates a unique Session ID and sends it to the browser as a temporary cookie. This is the foundation of modern login and authentication systems.



Authentication Flow
<?php
  session_start();
  
  // Simulating a login
  $_SESSION['username'] = 'AdminJohn';
  $_SESSION['is_logged_in'] = true;
  
  // Checking access
  if($_SESSION['is_logged_in']) {
    echo "Welcome to the secure dashboard, " . $_SESSION['username'];
  }
  
  // To log out: session_destroy();
?>

title: XML Parsers
content:
XML Parsers

Reading Structured Data

Before JSON became the industry standard, XML was the king of data transfer. Many legacy systems and RSS feeds still use it. PHP can easily read and manipulate XML data using built-in parser functions like simplexml_load_string().

SimpleXML
<?php
  $xmlData = "<note>
                <to>Tove</to>
                <from>Jani</from>
                <heading>Reminder</heading>
              </note>";

  // Load the string into an object
  $xml = simplexml_load_string($xmlData);
  
  // Access elements exactly like object properties!
  echo "Message to: " . $xml->to;
?>

title: File Upload
content:
PHP File Upload

Uploading Data

PHP allows you to upload files (like profile pictures or documents) from an HTML form directly to your server. The HTML form MUST use method="post" and include the enctype="multipart/form-data" attribute.



HTML Form Setup
<!-- This HTML sends the file to upload.php -->
<form action="upload.php" method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data">
  Select image to upload:
  <input type="file" name="fileToUpload" id="fileToUpload">
  <input type="submit" value="Upload Image" name="submit">
</form>

<!-- Inside upload.php, you use the $_FILES superglobal to save it: -->
<!-- move_uploaded_file($_FILES["fileToUpload"]["tmp_name"], $target_file); -->

title: Foreach Loop
content:
PHP Foreach Loop

Iterating Collections

The foreach loop works only on arrays and objects. It is the easiest way to loop through each key/value pair in an array without having to keep track of index numbers.

Looping Arrays
<?php
  $ages = array("Sanjith"=>20, "Alex"=>25, "Sarah"=>22);
  
  // Automatically loops through every item in the array
  foreach ($ages as $name => $age) {
    echo "Name: $name, Age: $age <br>";
  }
?>

title: Include & Require
content:
Include and Require

Modular Code

You can insert the content of one PHP file into another PHP file before the server executes it. This is how you create reusable headers, footers, and navigation menus across an entire website!



Differences
<?php
  // include will throw a Warning if the file is missing, but the script continues
  include 'header.php';
  
  // require will throw a Fatal Error and completely STOP the script if missing
  require 'database_config.php';
  
  echo "Main page content goes here...";
?>

title: Namespaces
content:
PHP Namespaces

Avoiding Collisions

In large applications (or when using third-party libraries), you might accidentally create a class with the same name as an existing class. Namespaces act like virtual folders to group related classes together and prevent these naming conflicts.



Declaring Namespaces
<?php
  // The namespace declaration MUST be the very first line in the file
  namespace HtmlUI;
  
  class Table {
    public $title = "";
    public $numRows = 0;
  }
  
  // To use it: $myTable = new HtmlUI\Table();
?>

title: Error Handling
content:
PHP Error Handling

Graceful Failures

Proper error handling prevents unexpected crashes and hides sensitive system paths from users. Use try...catch blocks to "catch" exceptions before they crash your application.

Try / Catch
<?php
  function divide($dividend, $divisor) {
    if($divisor == 0) {
      throw new Exception("Division by zero is illegal");
    }
    return $dividend / $divisor;
  }
  
  try {
    echo divide(5, 0);
  } catch(Exception $e) {
    echo "Error caught: " . $e->getMessage();
  }
?>

title: Constants
content:
PHP Constants

Unchanging Values

Constants are like variables, except that once they are defined, they cannot be changed or undefined. They are perfect for global configuration settings (like API keys or site names). They do not use the $ symbol.

Using define()
<?php
  // define(name, value, case-insensitive)
  define("SITE_NAME", "My Awesome Platform");
  
  // Alternatively, using the const keyword:
  const DB_USER = "root";
  
  echo "Welcome to " . SITE_NAME;
?>

title: Math & Date
content:
Math and Date Functions

Built-in Utilities

PHP comes with a massive library of utility functions right out of the box. You don't need to write complex algorithms for standard math or time conversions.

Utility Examples
<?php
  // Math functions
  echo "Absolute value: " . abs(-6.7) . "<br>";
  echo "Highest number: " . max(5, 10, 2, 99) . "<br>";
  
  // Date formatting (Y-m-d H:i:s is standard database format)
  echo "Current Server Time: " . date("Y-m-d H:i:s");
?>

title: JSON Handling
content:
PHP JSON Handling

Building APIs

This makes PHP perfect for building APIs. For example, if you are building a React Native mobile application like MealMitra, the frontend will request data from the server, and your PHP backend will reply by packaging the database results into JSON using json_encode()!

Encoding Data
<?php
  // A standard PHP Associative Array (perhaps fetched from a database)
  $userData = array("name" => "Sanjith", "role" => "Admin", "age" => 20);
  
  // Encode it into a JSON string so a mobile app or React frontend can read it
  $jsonString = json_encode($userData);
  
  echo $jsonString;
?>

title: Ultimate Summary
content:
PHP Final Summary 🚀

Course Completed! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the advanced PHP curriculum. You now have the skills to build highly complex, secure, and modular server-side architectures.

  • Data Handling: Superglobals, File Uploads, and deeply nested Multidimensional Arrays.
  • Architecture: OOP, Traits, Namespaces, and breaking code into Includes/Requires.
  • State & Security: Sessions, Cookies, and structured Error Handling.
  • API Communication: Seamlessly encoding and decoding JSON data.

You are officially equipped to build the backend of any modern web application or mobile API. Fantastic work!
